Metadata.Coram: Chan Sek Keong CJ; Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA; Judith Prakash J

Party_Details.Name: Ting Sing Ning (alias Malcolm Ding)

Facts.Text: Ting Sing Ning (alias Malcolm Ding) is a shareholder of Havilland Ltd holding 10% of the shares at all material times.
At all material times, Ting Sing Ning (alias Malcolm Ding) was one of four directors of Havilland Ltd together with Ting Chek Swee (alias Ting Chik Sui), Sia Cheng Yong and Binti.
On 20 March 2000, Ting Sing Ning commenced an action in Singapore for the benefit of Havilland Ltd against director Ting Chek Swee, with Sia Cheng Yong and Binti later added as defendants.
On 11 July 2000, Ting Sing Ning wrote to the board of directors of Havilland Ltd asking whether the board wished to adopt the action he had commenced on behalf of Havilland.
By early 2001, Ting Sing Ning had changed solicitors in the action concerning Havilland Ltd.
In early 2001, Ting Sing Ning applied to include directors Sia Cheng Yong and Binti as additional defendants in the action concerning Havilland Ltd, and this application was allowed in February 2001.
On 17 May 1999, during a meeting of Havilland Ltd stakeholders, Ting Sing Ning raised allegations of wrongdoing in relation to the affairs of Havilland.
On 16 February 2000, during a meeting of Havilland Ltd stakeholders, Ting Sing Ning raised allegations of wrongdoing in relation to the affairs of Havilland.
On 10 March 2000, during a meeting of Havilland Ltd stakeholders, Ting Sing Ning raised allegations of wrongdoing in relation to the affairs of Havilland.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a minority shareholder-director of Havilland Ltd could, on the basis of the shareholding structure and control of the company, pursue a common law derivative action on behalf of Havilland against other directors in respect of payments made to related companies.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the common law derivative action framework and the rule in Foss v Harbottle, an individual shareholder may pursue an action on behalf of a company where there is a prima facie case of wrongdoing affecting the company and the wrongdoers control or effectively control the company such that the company is unable or unlikely to initiate proceedings itself.
Party_Details.Application: Ting Sing Ning, as a 10% shareholder and director of Havilland Ltd, initiated proceedings in 2000 after raising concerns at several meetings in 1999 and 2000 and receiving no corrective action from the board or shareholders. The shareholding structure included his 10% interest, a 42% block held by three other directors, and an additional 10% held by a sister of one of those directors. The board and shareholders did not cause Havilland to commence proceedings despite being informed of the concerns, and the company did not adopt the action when invited to do so.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the appellant had locus standi to pursue a common law derivative action on behalf of Havilland Ltd. It accepted that the respondents had conceded a prima facie case of wrongdoing based on the forensic expert’s report. The central question became whether the alleged wrongdoers controlled Havilland or were in the seat of power, thereby preventing the company from suing. The court disagreed with the trial judge’s refusal to aggregate the 10% shareholding of Ting’s sister with the 42% held by Ting, Sia and Binti. It reasoned that, given the close familial relationship, the fact that Ting had given his sister enough shares to make her the largest shareholder of Merit, and her interest in Merit, there was a reasonable basis to conclude that she would support Ting’s group in any vote on whether Havilland should pursue the action. On this basis, the court treated the combined 52% as an absolute majority for control purposes. The court also examined the extraordinary general meeting of 13 March 2006, where shareholders voted against continuing the action. It noted that the appellant did not attend, but found that the shareholders were not fully apprised of the nature and substance of the allegations or the forensic expert’s affidavit. The court observed that the cause papers and expert affidavit were not made available to the shareholders, and that the explanation given at the meeting focused on procedural aspects and the appellant’s conduct rather than the detailed claims. It considered that the meeting appeared structured in a way that limited shareholders’ understanding of the alleged wrongdoing and that the appellant’s absence likely had no significant impact on the outcome. The court rejected the trial judge’s criticisms that the appellant had failed to put the matter before the board or shareholders and had not pursued the action diligently, pointing out that the appellant had raised his complaints at several meetings in 1999 and 2000, commenced proceedings in March 2000, wrote to the board in July 2000 to request adoption of the action, and that delays were partly due to attempts to serve Binti and changes of solicitors. The court further held that the availability of alternative remedies, such as winding up Havilland or pursuing an oppression action under Hong Kong law, did not justify refusing the derivative action in the circumstances, distinguishing Pang Yong Hock on the basis that there a prima facie case had not been made out. Concluding that the appellant fell within the fraud on the minority exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle and that the wrongdoers were in control or effectively in the seat of power, the Court of Appeal allowed the appeal and granted the appellant liberty to pursue the common law derivative action on behalf of Havilland to recover losses and seek an account of secret profits from the respondents.

Metadata.Coram: Chan Sek Keong CJ; Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA; Judith Prakash J

Party_Details.Name: Ting Chek Swee (alias Ting Chik Sui)

Facts.Text: At all material times, Ting Chek Swee (alias Ting Chik Sui) was a director of Havilland Ltd.
At all material times, Ting Chek Swee (alias Ting Chik Sui), together with Sia Cheng Yong and Binti, collectively held 42% of the shares in Havilland Ltd.
At all material times, Ting Chek Swee’s sister, Ting Shuk Choo, held 10% of the shares in Havilland Ltd.
At all material times, Havilland Ltd and Merit Concord Holdings had cross-shareholdings, with several shareholders of Havilland also holding shares in Merit.
By 2000, payments amounting to approximately $1.5 million had been made by Havilland Ltd to Merit Concord Holdings, a related company.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, given the shareholding structure of Havilland Ltd and the position of its directors and related shareholders, the group including Ting Chek Swee effectively controlled Havilland so as to prevent the company from initiating proceedings concerning payments to related companies.
Party_Details.Rule: For the fraud on the minority exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle to apply, the wrongdoers must be in control of the company, either through an absolute majority of voting power or through effective control such that they can prevent the company from deciding to pursue claims against them.
Party_Details.Application: Ting Chek Swee, together with two other directors, held 42% of Havilland Ltd’s shares, and his sister held an additional 10%. Several other shareholders of Havilland also held interests in Merit Concord Holdings, a company that had received substantial payments from Havilland. When the appellant requested that Havilland adopt the action, the board and shareholders did not cause the company to sue. An extraordinary general meeting in March 2006 resulted in a vote against continuing the action, with the directors in question abstaining from voting and other shareholders opposing continuation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered whether the group including Ting Chek Swee had control of Havilland for the purposes of the fraud on the minority exception. It rejected the trial judge’s view that the 10% shareholding of Ting’s sister should not be aggregated with the 42% held by Ting, Sia and Binti. The Court of Appeal reasoned that the sister’s position as the largest shareholder of Merit, her receipt of shares from Ting, and her interest in protecting Merit and Ting provided a substantial basis to treat her as aligned with Ting’s group. This produced an effective 52% voting block, which the court treated as an absolute majority. The court also examined the independence of other shareholders, noting their cross-shareholdings in Merit and the manner in which the EGM was conducted. It found that the shareholders were not fully informed of the detailed allegations or the forensic expert’s report, and that the meeting appeared structured to secure a vote against the action without substantive consideration of the claims. The court concluded that the wrongdoers were in control or effectively in the seat of power and had used that position to prevent Havilland from suing. On this basis, it held that the fraud on the minority exception applied and that the appellant could proceed with the derivative action against Ting and the other directors.

Metadata.Coram: Chan Sek Keong CJ; Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA; Judith Prakash J

Party_Details.Name: Sia Cheng Yong

Facts.Text: At all material times, Sia Cheng Yong was a director of Havilland Ltd.
At all material times, Sia Cheng Yong, together with Ting Chek Swee and Binti, held a total of 42% of the shares in Havilland Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of his shareholding and role as a director, together with the aligned shareholdings of other directors and related parties, Sia Cheng Yong formed part of a controlling group in Havilland Ltd that could prevent the company from pursuing claims concerning payments to related companies.
Party_Details.Rule: Control for the purposes of a common law derivative action includes situations where directors and their aligned shareholders hold or can command a majority of votes, or otherwise occupy the seat of power, such that they can block the company from deciding to sue in respect of wrongdoing.
Party_Details.Application: As one of three directors collectively holding 42% of Havilland Ltd’s shares, and in the context of an additional 10% held by the sister of a fellow director and cross-shareholdings in a related company that received substantial payments from Havilland, Sia Cheng Yong participated in board and shareholder processes where the company did not adopt the appellant’s action. He was involved in communicating shareholder opposition to adoption of the action and later in seeking a preliminary determination on the appellant’s standing.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated Sia Cheng Yong as part of the same group as Ting and Binti for the purpose of assessing control. It accepted that this group held 42% of Havilland’s shares and, when combined with the 10% held by Ting’s sister, effectively controlled 52%. The court considered that this group, together with shareholders with cross-shareholdings in Merit, occupied the seat of power in Havilland and was able to prevent the company from suing. The court noted that Sia joined Ting in applying for a preliminary issue on locus standi and in filing affidavits about the EGM where shareholders voted against continuing the action. It concluded that the group’s control and the way the EGM was conducted supported the application of the fraud on the minority exception, allowing the derivative action to proceed against Sia as well as the other directors.

Metadata.Coram: Chan Sek Keong CJ; Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA; Judith Prakash J

Party_Details.Name: Havilland Ltd

Facts.Text: Havilland Ltd is a company incorporated under the laws of Hong Kong with its principal place of business in Singapore.
At all material times, the directors of Havilland Ltd included the appellant, Ting Chek Swee (alias Ting Chik Sui), Sia Cheng Yong and Binti.
At all material times, shareholders of Havilland Ltd included the appellant with 10%, Ting Chek Swee, Sia Cheng Yong and Binti together with 42%, and Ting Shuk Choo with 10%.
Merit Concord Holdings was a related company to Havilland Ltd and was the fourth defendant in the derivative action commenced in 2000.
Havilland Ltd’s funds were allegedly used by Ting, Sia and Binti to finance Merit Concord Holdings, with the net amount claimed being $1,576,579.71.

On 31 July 2000, the board of Havilland Ltd sent a circular letter to all shareholders enclosing copies of affidavits filed in the action and asking whether they were in favour of Havilland adopting the action against director Ting.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Havilland Ltd, as a Hong Kong-incorporated company with its principal place of business in Singapore and a particular shareholding and board structure, was effectively controlled by directors and related shareholders in a way that prevented it from deciding independently to pursue claims concerning payments to related companies.
Party_Details.Rule: A company is treated as controlled by wrongdoers for the purposes of a common law derivative action when those individuals and their aligned shareholders hold or can command a majority of votes or otherwise occupy the seat of power, such that the company is unlikely to authorise proceedings against them.
Party_Details.Application: Havilland Ltd’s board and shareholding structure included a 42% block held by three directors and an additional 10% held by a sister of one of those directors, with other shareholders holding interests in a related company that had received substantial funds from Havilland. When invited in July 2000 to adopt the appellant’s action, the board and shareholders did not cause Havilland to sue. In March 2006, an extraordinary general meeting was convened at the request of shareholders, and the shareholders present or represented, other than the directors who abstained, voted unanimously against continuing the action.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated Havilland Ltd as the nominal corporate entity on whose behalf the derivative action was brought. It examined the company’s incorporation in Hong Kong, its principal place of business in Singapore, and its board and shareholding structure. The court noted that Havilland could not use the statutory derivative action under s 216A of the Singapore Companies Act because it was a Hong Kong company, and therefore the common law derivative action remained relevant. The court considered whether the company’s internal decision-making processes, including the July 2000 shareholder consultation and the March 2006 extraordinary general meeting, reflected an independent decision not to sue or were influenced by the alleged wrongdoers’ control. It concluded that the wrongdoers and their aligned shareholders effectively controlled Havilland and that the company’s failure to adopt the action did not preclude the appellant from proceeding derivatively on its behalf.
